preparation_method: virgin extraction from fresh coconut flesh

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard tablespoon of virgin coconut oil weighs approximately 14g and provides 124.9 kcal and 12.1g saturated fat — representing approximately 61% of the recommended daily saturated fat limit of 20g.

Input record uses 86.5g saturated fat consistent with virgin/cold-pressed variant values. Values accepted as verified. Monounsaturated fat (6.3g/100g) and polyunsaturated fat (1.7g/100g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC 171412.

gi_source.title: N/A — Glycemic index not applicable for pure fat foods with negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Virgin coconut oil is a pure fat (100g fat per 100g, 0g carbohydrate per 100g). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. No GI value exists or is meaningful for pure oils. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. Per Veritas protocol for zero-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high saturated fat content (86.5g/100g); 1 tablespoon (14g) provides 12.1g saturated fat — approximately 61% of the 20g daily saturated fat limit; lauric acid (47%) and myristic acid (18%) raise LDL cholesterol
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit or avoid coconut oil and replace it with extra-virgin olive oil or canola oil for daily cooking, as WHO, HPB Singapore, and AHA classify coconut oil as a saturated fat requiring restriction in cardiovascular risk management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High saturated fat intake is independently associated with insulin resistance and worsening of T2DM metabolic markers; the MCT marketing claim is misleading as lauric acid (47% of coconut oil) behaves metabolically as a long-chain fatty acid, not a true MCT
type2_diabetes.guidance: HPB Singapore explicitly advises T2DM patients to limit coconut oil and replace it with canola oil for stir-frying or extra-virgin olive oil for dressings, as no regulatory body supports the claimed MCT metabolic advantage at typical cooking doses of 1–2 tablespoons.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Coconut oil is the most saturated common cooking oil (86.5% saturated fat); myristic acid (18%) is the most potent LDL-raising saturated fatty acid; one tablespoon nearly reaches the AHA daily saturated fat limit of 13g (less than 6% of 2,000 kcal)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: WHO, AHA, HPB Singapore, and ESC consistently advise limiting coconut oil for patients with or at risk of cardiovascular disease; it should be replaced with unsaturated oils such as extra-virgin olive oil or canola oil, as no regulatory body endorses coconut oil as cardioprotective despite social media claims.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (86.5%) is pro-inflammatory at regular dietary doses; no clinical evidence supports anti-inflammatory benefit in RA at cooking doses despite in vitro lauric acid antibacterial properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Not recommended for RA patients seeking anti-inflammatory dietary support; replace with extra-virgin olive oil (oleocanthal anti-inflammatory effect) or canola oil (omega-3 ALA); occasional use in specific dishes is acceptable but daily cooking in coconut oil is not recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Virgin coconut oil (VCO) is cold-pressed oil extracted from fresh coconut flesh (Cocos nucifera) without chemical refining, bleaching, or deodorising — distinguishing it from refined coconut oil which undergoes solvent extraction and high-heat processing. It is the most saturated common cooking oil, containing 86.5g saturated fat per 100g — substantially higher than butter (approximately 51g), palm oil (approximately 49g), extra-virgin olive oil (approximately 14g), and canola oil (approximately 7g). The saturated fat profile is dominated by lauric acid (C12:0, approximately 47%), myristic acid (C14:0, approximately 18%), palmitic acid (C16:0, approximately 9%), and caprylic/capric acids (C8:0–C10:0, approximately 10%). One tablespoon (14g) provides approximately 124.9 kcal and 12.1g saturated fat — representing approximately 61% of the recommended daily saturated fat limit of 20g. Sensory profile: VCO has a characteristic mild coconut aroma and delicate sweet coconut flavour. It is white and solid at room temperature below approximately 24°C, and becomes a clear, light liquid when warmed above its melting point. It has a smooth, oily texture and a relatively high smoke point of approximately 177°C for unrefined VCO. Coconut oil is the traditional cooking fat of South and Southeast Asia, used for centuries in Singapore, Malaysia, India, the Philippines, and Sri Lanka for cooking, baking, traditional kuih preparation, and skincare and hair care in Ayurvedic and traditional medicine. In Singapore, VCO experienced a dramatic social media-driven health revival from approximately 2015 to 2022, with marketing claims about medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs) offering metabolic advantages including fat burning, weight loss, Alzheimer's prevention, and cardiovascular benefit. These claims are NOT supported by WHO, HPB Singapore, AHA, or ESC. The MCT claim is technically misleading: lauric acid (47% of coconut oil) is marketed as an MCT but is metabolised more like a long-chain saturated fatty acid than a true MCT. True MCTs (caprylic/capric acids) represent only approximately 15% of coconut oil content. Singapore's MOH and HPB explicitly advise limiting coconut oil for patients with hypertension, T2DM, or hypercholesterolaemia. For clinical populations, coconut oil should be replaced with canola oil or extra-virgin olive oil for daily cooking; occasional use for flavour in specific dishes such as kuih or curries is acceptable.
common_user_queries: is coconut oil good for diabetes

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171412), an approved primary source. Values are consistent with published literature on virgin coconut oil composition. Saturated fat profile (86.5g/100g) is consistent with USDA and peer-reviewed fatty acid composition data for cold-pressed VCO.
notes: protein_g is naturally zero as coconut oil is a pure plant-derived fat containing no protein fraction — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as coconut oil contains no carbohydrates — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. fat_g is 100.0g/100g as this is a pure oil product. Virgin coconut oil; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (pure fat; 0g carbohydrate per 100g); gi_category set to not_applicable per Veritas protocol for zero-carbohydrate foods. Monounsaturated fat (6.3g/100g) and polyunsaturated fat (1.7g/100g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC 171412. The input record description noted 82.5g saturated fat in the description text but 86.5g in the per_100g data field; the per_100g data value of 86.5g is used as it is consistent with USDA SR Legacy values for virgin/cold-pressed coconut oil and is the machine-readable authoritative value. The input record contained a duplicate portion entry (two identical 14g tablespoon entries); deduplicated to one entry. The input aliases incorrectly assigned Chinese-script aliases (椰子油) to language code 'en'; corrected to 'chinese' in aliases_by_language. FoodEx2 code A00LC assigned for coconut oil based on EFSA FoodEx2 classification system; SNOMED CT code 226365003 assigned for coconut oil substance. water_g set to 0.0 as pure oil contains negligible water — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for virgin coconut oil. Aliases self-check performed: every string value in aliases_by_language verified present verbatim in flat aliases array — all 20 entries confirmed consistent.
